title: Miami (OH) vs Tennessee — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • Philadelphia, PA
venue: Xfinity Mobile Arena, Philadelphia, PA • 4:25 PM ET • TBS
extraInfo: Winner faces #3 Virginia or #14 Wright State in the Round of 32

stakes.callout: The team that nearly went undefeated meets an SEC power that can't stop losing close games. Miami (OH) just survived the First Four — beating SMU on Wednesday to improve to 32-1 — and now faces a Tennessee squad that has lost 4 of its last 6. The RedHawks have the nation's best record and the most efficient offense in the MAC. Tennessee has a projected lottery pick and back-to-back Elite Eight appearances. Something has to give.

cards.label: For Miami (OH)
cards.value: The first tournament win since 1999, when Wally Szczerbiak turned March into "Wally's World." They beat SMU on Wednesday and now have a chance to make the Round of 32 for the first time in 27 years. A 32-1 record demands validation against a major-conference opponent.

cards.label: For Tennessee
cards.value: Rick Barnes has never made a Final Four — in 11 years at Tennessee or 17 at Texas. After back-to-back Elite Eights, a first-round loss to an 11-seed would be devastating. The Vols need to snap out of their late-season slide immediately.

cards.label: The B.J. Edwards Ghost
cards.value: B.J. Edwards transferred from Tennessee to SMU — but was ruled out with an ankle injury for the First Four and never got to face his old team. SMU lost to Miami. The reunion that never happened adds a layer of narrative intrigue.

cards.label: The Fatigue Factor
cards.value: Miami played two days ago in Dayton. Tennessee hasn't played since Saturday. The RedHawks may be running on adrenaline or running on fumes — quick turnarounds in March can go either way.

styles.title: Six Guys Score in Double Figures
bullets: Offensive firepower: 90.7 PPG (2nd nationally) with the nation's best FG% at 52.4%. Offensive efficiency of 123.9 ranks 4th nationally. Six players average double figures — this isn't a one-man show.
Ball movement: Just 10.5 turnovers per game. Miami creates quality looks through unselfish play, not isolation. The offense runs through pick-and-roll and swing-swing ball movement.
Clutch factor: 8-0 in one-possession games this season. They just erased a deficit to beat SMU in the First Four. This team doesn't panic.
Defense is the weakness: 75.3 PPG allowed (223rd nationally), KenPom defensive efficiency ranks 156th. They generate steals (7.4/game) and blocks (3.3/game) but can be scored on.
The question: KenPom ranks them 91st despite 32 wins. Strength of schedule: 274th. Zero Quad 1 wins before the SMU game. They dominated inferior competition — can it work against Tennessee's talent?

styles.title: Elite Defense, Shaky Closer
bullets: Defense first: KenPom 15th in adjusted defensive efficiency (95.0). Opponents shoot just .409 from the field (32nd nationally). Felix Okpara anchors the interior with 1.7 blocks per game.

Two-star backcourt: Ja'Kobi Gillespie (18.0 PPG, First Team All-SEC) and Nate Ament (17.5 PPG, Julius Erving Award finalist) combine for 35.5 PPG. When both are healthy, Tennessee can outscore anyone.
Offensive rebounding: 2nd nationally at 16.0 offensive rebounds per game. Tennessee will get second and third chances against Miami's mediocre defense.
Crunch-time concerns: Lost 4 of last 6, all by 7 points or fewer. Got blown out 69-47 by Houston in the SEC semifinals. Quad 1 record of 5-9. Closing games has been a problem.
The Ament question: The projected lottery pick sprained his ankle February 24, missed two weeks, and returned for the SEC Tournament. He scored 27 against Auburn — but is he truly 100%?

keys.title: Can Miami's offense survive the SEC upgrade?
keys.description: The RedHawks led the nation in FG% at 52.4% — but they did it against the MAC and one ACC team in SMU. Tennessee's defense ranks 15th nationally in KenPom and holds opponents to .409 shooting. If Miami shoots below 45%, their formula breaks. If they can sustain even 48%, they have a real chance.

keys.title: Tennessee's offensive rebounding vs. Miami's weak interior.
keys.description: Tennessee ranks 2nd nationally with 16.0 offensive rebounds per game. Miami allows 75+ PPG and ranks 156th in defensive efficiency. Okpara, Carey, and Estrella will create second-chance opportunities. If Tennessee dominates the glass, they can overcome cold shooting stretches.

keys.title: Is Nate Ament healthy?
keys.description: The projected lottery pick sprained his ankle on February 24 and missed two weeks. He returned and scored 27 against Auburn in the SEC Tournament — but Tennessee went 1-2 in those three games. If he's limited, Tennessee becomes a one-star team with Gillespie. If he's fully healthy, the Vols have two players Miami has no answer for.

keys.title: The First Four hangover — or First Four momentum?
keys.description: Miami played Wednesday night in Dayton and now flies to Philadelphia for a Friday afternoon tip. Tennessee has been resting since Saturday. Quick turnarounds in March are unpredictable — sometimes the team with momentum rides it; sometimes fatigue catches up. Watch Miami's legs in the second half.

keys.title: Tennessee's closing problem.
keys.description: The Vols lost 4 of their last 6, all by 7 points or fewer. In close games, they've struggled to execute down the stretch. Miami is 8-0 in one-possession games. If this game is tight with 5 minutes left, Miami's composure could be the difference against a Tennessee team that has been tightening up.

prediction.summary: Miami's 52.4% shooting meets Tennessee's top-15 defense, and that's the whole ballgame. The RedHawks built their 32-1 record by getting clean looks against MAC defenses — but Okpara's rim protection and Tennessee's length will make those looks harder to come by. The X-factor is fatigue: Miami played two days ago in Dayton, and Tennessee has been resting since Saturday. If Suder, Elmer, and Byers can shoot even 46-47% against Tennessee's defense, the RedHawks' composure in close games (8-0 in one-possession games) makes this dangerous. But Gillespie and Ament are a different caliber of scorer than anything Miami has seen, and Tennessee's offensive rebounding (2nd nationally) will generate extra possessions that a tired Miami team can't afford to give up.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Tennessee's defense sets the tone, contesting everything and holding Miami below 40% shooting early. Gillespie gets going with two quick baskets. But Miami's ball movement keeps them within striking distance — Suder finds a couple open threes, and the RedHawks trail by only 3-4.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: Ament starts asserting himself inside, drawing fouls and getting to the line. Okpara and Carey dominate the offensive glass, giving Tennessee second-chance points. Miami's efficiency dips below 45% as the defensive upgrade from MAC opponents becomes clear. Tennessee takes a 7-9 point lead into halftime.

phases.title: Second Half Push
phases.description: Miami comes out with a burst — Skaljac and Byers hit back-to-back threes, and suddenly it's a 3-point game. The RedHawks' poise in tight moments shows. Tennessee starts tightening up, and their late-season closing problems resurface. With 8 minutes left, it's anyone's game.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: This is where the First Four fatigue catches up. Miami's legs go, and their shooting percentages crater. Tennessee's offensive rebounding creates a critical putback, and Gillespie hits a dagger mid-range jumper. The Vols survive, but Miami makes them earn every inch.

projectedScore.rationale: Tennessee's defensive talent and offensive rebounding edge overcome Miami's composure and efficiency, with fatigue from the First Four ultimately sapping the RedHawks in the final stretch.

players.name: Peter Suder
players.meta: G • Senior • MAC Player of the Year • Carmel, IN
players.bio:
Transferred from Bellarmine, where he averaged 10 PPG. Won two Indiana state championships at Carmel High School. Now the 7th player in Miami history to win MAC Player of the Year. Dropped 37 points in a 105-102 thriller vs. Buffalo on New Year's Day. Shoots .554 from the floor and .429 from three — an elite efficiency combination.
Read more →

players.name: Eian Elmer
players.bio: His first D-I offer came from Northern Kentucky. Coach Steele signed him in his first recruiting class, and Elmer has developed year by year: 7.0 PPG as a freshman, 11.0 as a sophomore, now 12.6 as a junior. Sealed his high school state championship with a two-handed dunk. Shoots .434 from three while being the team's best rebounder and defender.

players.name: Luke Skaljac
players.meta: G • Sophomore • Ohio Mr. Basketball Finalist • Broadview Heights, OH
players.bio:
When starter Evan Ipsaro tore his ACL in December, the undefeated season could have unraveled. Instead, Skaljac stepped in and Miami never lost — averaging 13.3 PPG as a starter with double figures in 9 of 11 starts. His dad and three brothers all played at the same Ohio high school. Coach Steele recruited him as "the best guard in the state of Ohio."
Read more →

players.name: Brant Byers
players.bio:
From a small Pennsylvania town that rarely produces D-I players. His prep school shut down; he transferred to Perkiomen School and called it "a wide awakening." Redshirted at Miami, came off the bench, and now starts on the winningest team in the country. Profiled by Basket Under Review as: "Brant Byers is 'simple,' but he's become a crucial piece of college basketball's best story."
Read more →

players.name: Ja'Kobi Gillespie
players.meta: G • 6-2 • Senior • Greeneville, TN • First Team All-SEC
players.bio:
The ultimate homecoming story. Grew up in Greeneville, Tennessee — the first player from northeast Tennessee named Tennessee Mr. Basketball in the award's 37-year history. Played at Belmont and Maryland before coming home: "I always told my dad I wanted to play for Tennessee." He took less NIL money to do it. Scored a career-high 34 against Texas. Named AP SEC Newcomer of the Year. Raises money for Make-A-Wish with every three he makes.
Read more →

players.name: Nate Ament
players.meta: F • 6-10 • Freshman • Warrenton, VA • Julius Erving Award Finalist
players.bio:
The No. 4 recruit in the 2025 class and a projected top-10 NBA Draft pick. Only the third top-five recruit in Tennessee history (after Tobias Harris and Allan Houston). Scored 27 points with 8 rebounds, 4 assists, and 3 blocks in a comeback SEC Tournament win over Auburn. Sprained his ankle February 24 and missed two weeks — his health is the biggest X-factor in Tennessee's March.
Read more →

players.name: Felix Okpara
players.meta: C • 6-11 • Senior • Lagos, Nigeria • SEC All-Defensive Team
players.bio:
Born in Lagos, Nigeria. Played soccer as a defensive midfielder until age 14, when he moved to the U.S. having never played basketball. Transferred from Ohio State, where he started 45 games. Scored a co-career-high 20 points on 8 dunks at South Carolina and went 7-of-7 from the field at Missouri. His rim protection extends Tennessee's streak of SEC All-Defensive Team members to seven consecutive seasons.
Read more →

players.name: Jaylen Carey
players.meta: F • 6-8, 245 lbs • Junior • Davie, FL • Vanderbilt Transfer
players.bio:
The first player ever to transfer between Vanderbilt and Tennessee in men's basketball. Brother of former Duke All-American and NBA player Vernon Carey Jr.; father played 8 NFL seasons with the Dolphins. When Vandy fans heckled him on his return, he didn't flinch: "I don't care about no black-and-gold." Provides interior toughness at 245 pounds.
Read more →

funFacts.title: The Cradle of Coaches (Miami OH)
funFacts.body: Miami is the only school with 3 Super Bowl-winning head coaches among its alumni — John Harbaugh, Sean McVay, and Weeb Ewbank. 11 bronze statues stand at the Cradle of Coaches Plaza. More →

funFacts.title: Rocky Top (Tennessee)
funFacts.body: Tennessee's signature song isn't actually the fight song — it's a bluegrass standard written by Felice and Boudleaux Bryant in 1967 at the Gatlinburg Inn. USA Today named it the #1 fight song in college football. Peyton Manning popularized the tradition of conducting the marching band after big wins.

funFacts.title: The Speedo Promise (Miami OH)
funFacts.body: On ESPN College GameDay, coach Travis Steele promised to wear a Speedo on Selection Sunday if Miami finished undefeated and won the MAC Tournament. The swim team had been showing up to games in Speedos, caps, and goggles. Reports say he honored the promise anyway.

funFacts.title: Smokey the Coonhound (Tennessee)
funFacts.body: In 1953, a Bluetick Coonhound named "Blue Smokey" was entered in a halftime mascot contest. When his name was called, he howled. The crowd roared. He howled again. The students voted. Smokey has been Tennessee's live mascot ever since. More →

funFacts.title: Gillespie Came Home (Tennessee)
funFacts.body: Ja'Kobi Gillespie grew up in Greeneville, Tennessee, dreaming of playing for the Vols. After stops at Belmont and Maryland, he took less NIL money to come home. Rick Barnes called him "probably the best value in all of college basketball."

funFacts.title: The Ernie & Bernie Show (Tennessee)
funFacts.body: Ernie Grunfeld and Bernard King combined to average 40+ PPG from 1974-77, earning a Sports Illustrated cover and an ESPN 30 for 30. Both were first-round NBA picks. King became a 4x All-Star.

funFacts.title: The DJ Incident (Miami OH)
funFacts.body: During a road win that pushed Miami to 29-0, Steele shoved "The Most Incredible DJ Chuck's" equipment onto the floor at halftime. Fined $2,500 by the MAC. He called to apologize and Miami reimbursed the $3,000 in damages.

funFacts.title: Lifetime Contract (Tennessee)
funFacts.body: Rick Barnes, 71, signed a lifetime contract in August 2025 — $6 million per year that automatically extends annually. He's 28 wins shy of passing Ray Mears as Tennessee's all-time winningest coach. He's never made a Final Four.

funFacts.title: Lagos to Knoxville (Tennessee)
funFacts.body: Felix Okpara was born in Lagos, Nigeria, and never played basketball until age 14. He was a soccer midfielder. Now he's the SEC All-Defensive Team anchor who scored 20 points on 8 dunks at South Carolina.

funFacts.title: Famous Alumni Showdown
funFacts.body: Miami: Ben Roethlisberger (2x Super Bowl QB), Paul Ryan (Speaker of the House). Tennessee: Peyton Manning (NFL legend), Cormac McCarthy (Pulitzer Prize), Paul Rudd... wait, that's Kansas. Tennessee has Candace Parker and the co-founder of Garmin.

funFacts.title: The Vanderbilt Defector (Tennessee)
funFacts.body: Jaylen Carey became the first player to transfer between Vanderbilt and Tennessee in men's basketball history. When Vandy fans heckled him, he replied: "I don't care about no black-and-gold." His brother Vernon Jr. was a Duke All-American.

funFacts.title: "Wally's World" (Miami OH)
funFacts.body: The last time Miami won an NCAA Tournament game was 1999, when Wally Szczerbiak scored 43 points against Washington and 24 to upset #2 Utah, reaching the Sweet 16. He was drafted 6th overall that June.

history.callout: Two programs at opposite ends of the NCAA Tournament spectrum. Tennessee has made 28 tournament appearances, reached the Elite Eight in back-to-back years, and has never made a Final Four. Miami (OH) hasn't won a tournament game since Wally Szczerbiak's legendary 1999 run — until they beat SMU on Wednesday. Now they're looking for more.

timeline.text: Wally Szczerbiak scored 43 points in a 59-58 Miami victory. A 10-seed knocking off a 7.
Szczerbiak added 24 points as Miami upset the national runners-up. First time in program history they'd won two tournament games.
The run ended against the defending champions. Szczerbiak scored 23 in defeat. He was drafted 6th overall by Minnesota that June.

subsections.title: Tennessee's Quest for a First Final Four

timeline.text: Under Bruce Pearl, Tennessee reached the Elite Eight for the first time. Lost to Michigan State.
Rick Barnes' best regular-season team earned a #1 seed in the South. Lost in the Sweet 16 to Purdue in overtime.
As a #2 seed, Tennessee reached the Elite Eight again. Lost to Houston 69-50. So close, yet again.
Another #2 seed, another Elite Eight — and another loss. The program still has zero Final Four appearances in 28 tournament trips.

subsections.title: The Volunteer Tradition
subsections.content: The "Volunteers" nickname traces to the War of 1812, when 1,500 Tennesseans fought under Andrew Jackson at the Battle of New Orleans. During the Mexican-American War, the governor called for 2,600 volunteers and Tennessee sent more than 30,000. The name stuck. It's fitting that a program with this tradition of showing up has made 8 straight NCAA Tournaments under Barnes — even if the ultimate prize has eluded them.
